coredata.dc:title: Color tunable carbon quantum dots from wasted paper by different solvents for anti-counterfeiting and fluorescent flexible film
coredata.dc:creator: Park, Sung Jun
Park, Jin Young
Chung, Jong Won
Yang, Hyun Kyoung
Moon, Byung Kee
Yi, Soung Soo
coredata.dc:description: Currently, the disposal of municipal wastes (paper, plastic, and coffee residue) is a problem due to increasing the amount of municipal wastes, global environmental problems, and high disposal cost. Here, we report the synthesis of carbon quantum dots from waste paper by different solvents by a conventional hydro-/solvothermal method for solving the environmental and disposal problems. The carbon quantum dots synthesized with waste papers have several advantages such as low toxicity, low cost, and eco-friendly. The prepared carbon quantum dots have main emission peak from 440 to 540 nm under 340–480 nm excitation. The prepared carbon quantum dots can be applied to easily prepare anti-counterfeiting ink and fluorescent flexible film because of their unique optical property and excellent chemical and photostability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Waste paper
Municipal wastes
Anti-counterfeiting
Fluorescent film

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of D-, E-, and P-CQDs
Preparation of the anti-counterfeiting ink and film.
Preparation of the fluorescent flexible film

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea funded by the Ministry of Education ( 2017R1C1B1002434 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the D-, E-, and P-CQDs prepared at different solvents (a) DI-water, (c) EtOH, and (e) 2-PrOH, (inset) HR-TEM images. Size distributions of the (b, d, f) D-, E-, P-CQDs.
High-resolution XPS ((a, d, and g) C 1s, (b, e, and h) O 1s, and (c, f, and i) Ca 2p of D-, E-, and P-CQDs.
(a) FT-IR, (b) XRD, and (c) UV–Visible spectra of D-, E-, and P-CQDs. The PL spectra of (d) D-CQDs, (e) E-CQDs, and (f) P-CQDs upon excitation with different wavelengths.

Photographs of anti-counterfeiting mark with (a) E-CQDs on credit card and 50000-Won banknote (Korea) and anti-counterfeiting mark with (b) E-CQDs film on credit card and 50000-Won banknote (Korea) under the (i) day light, (ii) UV light (365 nm), and (iii) blue light (405 nm), respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Photographs of (a) written (i) alphabet (“K”) on credit card and (ii) circle on 50000-Won banknote (Korea) and (b) film on (i) credit card and (ii) 50000-Won banknote (Korea) under UV light (365 nm) and blue light (405 nm) for different times (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 h). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Photographs of the prepared D-CQDs/C-CQDs solutions with different D-CQDs/C-CQDs ratios (1:0, 19:1, 9:1, 4:1, 2:1, and 0:1) under day light and UV light (360 nm), (b) PL spectra of the prepared D-CQDs/C-CQDs solutions under 360 nm excitation, (c) CIE chromaticity coordinate of D-CQDs/C-CQDs solutions, and (d) photographs of D-CQDs/C-CQDs films with different D-CQDs/C-CQDs ratios (i. 1:0, ii. 19:1, iii. 9:1, iv. 4:1, v. 2:1, and vi. 0:1) under day light and UV light (360 nm).
Schematic illustration. (a) global paper flow, (b) global paper production, (c) fabrication of carbon dot using waste paper, and (d) several applications using the prepared carbon dot.

head.ce:title: Color tunable carbon quantum dots from wasted paper by different solvents for anti-counterfeiting and fluorescent flexible film

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of LED Convergence Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan 48547, Republic of Korea
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of LED Convergence Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan 48547, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electrical, Electronics, and Software Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan 48513, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electrical, Electronics, and Software Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department Electrical, Electronics, and Software Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan 48513, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Pukyong National University, Busan 48513, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Silla University, Busan 46958, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Silla University, Busan 46958, Republic of Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Departement of Electrical, Electronics, and Software Engineering, Pukyong National University, Busan 48513, Republic of Korea.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Departement of Electrical, Electronics, and Software Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Currently, the disposal of municipal wastes (paper, plastic, and coffee residue) is a problem due to increasing the amount of municipal wastes, global environmental problems, and high disposal cost. Here, we report the synthesis of carbon quantum dots from waste paper by different solvents by a conventional hydro-/solvothermal method for solving the environmental and disposal problems. The carbon quantum dots synthesized with waste papers have several advantages such as low toxicity, low cost, and eco-friendly. The prepared carbon quantum dots have main emission peak from 440 to 540 nm under 340–480 nm excitation. The prepared carbon quantum dots can be applied to easily prepare anti-counterfeiting ink and fluorescent flexible film because of their unique optical property and excellent chemical and photostability.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been much researched in the past decade due to their excellent optical properties (strong resistance to photobleaching, tunable excitation, and emission), high photochemical, and colloidal stability, good biocompatibility, facile synthesis process, low cost of raw materials, and eco-friendly [1–6]. According to these advantages, CQDs have been widely applied for CQDs based light-emitting diode, fluorescence sensing, biological imaging, catalysis, drug delivery, latent fingerprint detection, and anti-counterfeiting [7–13].

Counterfeiting of valuable items such as currency, confidential documents, pharmaceuticals, and jewelry has become a global problem because it badly affects social economy [14–17]. Therefore, the development of anti-counterfeiting technique is needed. Up till now, several methods for preventing the counterfeiting include luminescent labels with using luminescent materials (inorganic phosphors, quantum dots, organic dyes, etc.) [18–20]. However, the inorganic phosphors have several disadvantages such as complex synthesis and expensive starting material. The quantum dots include heavy metals which induce bad influence to humans and environment. In the case of the organic dyes, thermal, photo, and chemical stability is lower than other materials. To overcome the above disadvantages, we applied to the anti-counterfeiting with using CQDs.

Recently, the researches on recycling of municipal wastes (coffee residue, paper, commercial materials, plastic, glass, etc.) have been conducted due to severe global environmental problems and high disposal cost [21–23]. The papers, one of the major causes of municipal wastes, have become a problem. Generally, it is well known that most of the discarded papers is recycled as new papers through the conventional paper recycling. However, in reality, the used papers about 40% are abandoned and the rest is recycled as shown in Scheme 1 (a) [22]. The global paper production has grown over the past few decades because of its important use (packaging, sanitary, printing, writing, and newspaper) as depicted in Scheme 1(b) [22,24]. It causes that the amount of discarded papers increases. To solve this problem, the development of applications for recycling waste paper has been needed. As known to all, the main constituent of paper after the pulping process is cellulose. The other components (lignin, hemicellulose, etc) are eliminated through pulping process by several chemical reactions. It means that waste paper including the cellulose can serve as a carbon source. In this paper, we report the method to solve disposal and environmental pollution through recycling the waste paper.

In the present work, the CQDs were synthesized through one-pot hydro-/solvothermal method using waste paper as environmental friendliness carbon sources with different solvents (deionized water, ethanol, and 2-propanol) as shown in Scheme 1(c). The fabricated CQDs and films have good optical property and photo and chemical stability. Therefore, we present the applicability of anti-counterfeiting ink and fluorescent flexible film with using the prepared CQDs as shown in Scheme 1(d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of D-, E-, and P-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: 0.5 g of waste paper as carbon source was put in 40 mL of deionized water (DI-water: D-CQDs), ethanol (EtOH: E-CQDs), and 2-propanol (2-PrOH: P-CQDs), respectively. The mixture was transferred into a stainless steel autoclave with a Teflon-lined liner (60 mL capacity) and heated at 210 °C with magnetic stirring for obtaining homogeneous solution. After cooling down to room temperature (25 °C) naturally, the obtained solutions were filtered through a 0.2 μm membrane filter (CHM, Spain) to remove large aggregates. The products were purified using a 3500 Da dialysis bag (Spectra/Por®6, molecular weight cutoff 3500 Da, nominal flat width 45 mm, diameter 29 mm, USA) for 2 d to remove the unreacted small molecules. After filtration using membrane filter, the D-, E-, and P-CQDs were obtained.

2 g of Citric acid (CA) as carbon source and 2 g of urea were dispersed in 20 mL of N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF). The mixture was transferred into a stainless steel autoclave with a Teflon-lined liner (60 mL capacity) and heated at 160 °C with magnetic stirring for obtaining homogeneous solution. After cooling down to room temperature (25 °C) naturally, the obtained solution was filtered through a 0.2 μm membrane filter (CHM, Spain) to remove large aggregates. The product was purified using a 3500 Da dialysis bag (Spectra/Por®6, molecular weight cutoff 3500 Da, nominal flat width 45 mm, diameter 29 mm, USA) for 2 d to remove the unreacted small molecules. After filtration using membrane filter, the yellow emitting C-CQDs was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the anti-counterfeiting ink and film.
ce:section.ce:para: 2 g of Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, average molecular weight: 500) was dissolved in 30 mL deionized water (DI-water) under stirring using a magnetic stirrer at 90 °C until a transparent solution was obtained, which was then cooled to room temperature (RT). Appropriate amounts of PVA solution was mixed with the prepared E-CQDs under stirring using a magnetic stirrer at RT for 30 min until the mixture was well mixed. The resultant E-CQDs solutions (E-CQDs + PVA solution) were employed as anti-counterfeiting ink for information encryption.

The anti-counterfeiting film was prepared as followed. 5 g of Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, average molecular weight: 500) was dissolved in 50 mL deionized water (DI-water) under stirring using a magnetic stirrer at 90 °C until a transparent solution was obtained, which was then cooled to RT. Appropriate amounts of PVA solution was mixed with the prepared E-CQDs under stirring using a magnetic stirrer at RT for 30 min until the mixture was well mixed. The E-CQDs solutions were dropped into a petri dish with using a pipette and dried at 60 °C for 1 day. High transparent and fluorescent flexible films were obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the fluorescent flexible film
ce:section.ce:para: 5 g of Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, average molecular weight: 1500) was dissolved in 50 mL deionized water (DI-water) under vigorous stirring using a magnetic stirrer at 90 °C until a transparent solution was obtained, which was then cooled to RT. Appropriate amounts of PVA solution was mixed with prepared CQDs with different D-CQDs/C-CQDs ratios (1:0, 19:1, 9:1, 4:1, 2:1, and 0:1) under stirring. The D-CQDs/C-CQDs solutions were dropped into a petri dish with using a pipette and dried at 60 °C for 1 day. High transparent and fluorescent flexible films were obtained.

The morphology of CQDs was examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, HITACHI LTD, H-7500) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of CQDs was investigated by a PHI 5000 VersaProbe spectrometer using a monochromatic Al Kα radiation source. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra of CQDs were measured by using a Perkin-Elmer spectrophotometer. UV–Vis spectra of CQDs have been recorded on a (V-670, JASCO). The structure of CQDs in D2O was examined by Fourier transform nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer (NMR, JEOL, JNM ECP-400). The luminescence properties of the prepared CQDs and films were observed at room temperature by using a luminescence spectrophotometer (Photon Technology International (PTI)) with a 60 W Xe-arc lamp. The structure of the prepared CQDs was obtained by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using an X’PERT PRO X-ray diffractometer using CuKα radiation (1.5406 Å) at a beam voltage of 40 kV and beam current of 30 mA.

The morphological and size of the D-, E-, and P-CQDs are observed by TEM and size distribution as shown in Fig. 1 . In Fig. 1(a, c, e), the wide area TEM images of the D-, E-, and P-CQDs represent well-dispersed and uniform in size distribution. The average particle sizes of the D-, E-, and P-CQDs are about 2.6, 4.0, and 4.4 nm, respectively. The average particle sizes of the D-, E-, and P-CQDs gradually increased from 2.6 to 4.4 nm which caused red-shifted emission colors because of the quantum confinement effect. The HR-TEM images (inset) of the D-, E-, and P-CQDs represent similar well-resolved lattice fringes (with a d spacing of 0.21 nm) corresponding to the (1 0 0) inter-planar spacing of the graphitic carbon.

To examine the composition and chemical environmental, the XPS survey spectra of the D-, E-, and P-CQDs show four predominant peaks at 285.7 eV, 346.6 eV, 437.4 eV, and 531.1 eV for C 1s, Ca 2p, Ca 2s, and O 1s, respectively as shown in Fig. S1. It indicates that all CQDs have the same elements. The high-resolution XPS spectra of the D-, E-, and P-CQDs are depicted in Fig. 2 (a, d, g: C 1s), (b, e, h: O 1s), and (c, f, i: Ca 2p), respectively. The C 1s bands were split into three distinct Gaussian peaks centered at 284.9 eV, 286.5 eV, and 288.6 eV, which are assigned to graphitic sp2 C (CC/CC), COH, and carbonyl C (CO) bands, respectively.[25] The O 1s bands were deconvoluted into two Gaussian peaks centered at 531.7 eV and 532.8 eV for CO and COH/COC bands, respectively.[26] The Ca 2p bands consist of two Gaussian peaks centered at 347.5 eV and 351.0 eV for Ca 2p1/2 and Ca 2p3/2 bands, respectively.

As shown in Fig. 3 (a), the FT-IR spectra show the chemical compositions and functional groups of the D-, E-, and P-CQDs. The stretching band of OH was observed at 3349 cm−1. Peaks at 2973 cm−1, 1637 cm−1, 1380 cm−1, and 1050 cm−1 are corresponding to CH stretching vibration, CO stretching vibration, COC anti-symmetric stretching vibration, and stretching vibration of CC anti-symmetric stretching vibration, and stretching O bonds in carboxyl groups.[27,28] These results show that the prepared CQDs contain abundant polar functional groups, such as OH, CO, COC, and CO, indicating that the D-, E-, and P-CQDs have high solubility in polar solvents. The XRD patterns of the D-, E-, and P-CQDs show a broad peak at around 22°, which confirms the graphene structure of the D-, E-, and P-CQDs as shown in Fig. 3(b). Around 44°, there is a weak peak, which is graphitic carbon [29].

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the D-, E-, and P-CQDs are shown in Fig. 3(c). The D-, E-, and P-CQDs have one absorption peak about 350 nm which is attributed to n-π* transition of C = O caused by several moieties [30,31]. Comparing the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the D-, E-, and P-GQDs, the absorption peak has a slight red shift. It indicates that this peak is affected by the chemical composition [Ref. [31].

The 1H NMR spectrum (D2O, ppm) of E-CQDs shows clearly observed peaks in the range of 1–3 and 3–4.5 ppm, which correspond to the sp3 C–H proton and protons attached with ether, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups as shown in Fig. S2(a) [32]. Fig. S2(b) shows the 13C NMR spectrum of E-CQDs which has several peaks in the range of 20–80 (sp3 carbons and carbons attached with hydroxyl groups and ether linkage) and 175–185 ppm (sp2 CO).[33]

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of D-, E-, and P-CQDs are displayed in Fig. 3(d), (e), and (f). The emission peak position of the D-, E-, and P-CQDs is red-shift under different excitation wavelengths. It can be explained by the radiative recombination of the excited electrons from the n–π* transitions of CO in multiple surface emissive trap states.[34] The presence of carbon in sp3 state can be confirmed through the 13C NMR (Fig. S2(b)). The multiple electronic structures caused energy transitions between bandgaps and a pathway to radiative recombination which gives CQDs the special optical property responsive to different wavelength excitation.[35] Therefore, the prepared CQDs have the excitation dependent property. When the D-, E-, and P-CQDs are excited at wavelengths between 340 and 480 nm in increments of 20 nm, the corresponding main emission peaks are red-shift from 440 to 540 nm. The PL spectra of the D-CQDs show strong blue fluorescence and E-, and P-CQDs show cyan fluorescence around 435 nm under 360 nm excitation. The quantum yield (QY) was calculated using the following equation:

Q Y =
QY
ST
×
K X
/
K
RE
∗
η X
/
η
RE
2
, where
QY
ST
is the quantum yield of the rhodamine B dye as a reference,
K X
and
K
RE
are the linear slope from the plot of the integrated photoluminescence intensity versus absorption,
η X
and
η
RE
are the refractive index. From this equation, the QY of D-, E-, and P-CQDs is obtained about 12%, 27%, and 10%. Fig. S4 exhibits the CIE coordinates of the D-, E-, and P-CQDs under 360 nm and 440 nm. The CIE coordinates show 1 (0.1657, 0.1228), 2 (0.1877, 0.1952), and 3 (0.2020, 0.2238) under 360 nm and 1 (0.2294, 0.4029), 2 (0.2392, 0.4035), and 3 (0.2533, 0.4054) under 440 nm. Through these values, the red shifted fluorescence of the prepared CQDs with different solvents can be confirmed.

In order to confirm the applicability of the E-CQDs as anti-counterfeiting ink and anti-counterfeiting film, we wrote the alphabets (“PKNU”) on credit card and circle on a 50000-Won note (Korean currency) and put a film on credit card and a 50000-Won note (Korean currency). Fig. 4 (ai, bi) shows the written characters and film which are invisible under day light. When UV lamp (365 nm) was irradiated on the written characters and film, cyan-emitted characters and film from the E-CQDs were observed as shown in Fig. 4(aii, bii). They are similar to the existing anti-counterfeiting mark on the credit card (the white character “V”) and banknote (the green image “leaf”). Upon excitation with blue light (405 nm) radiation, green-emitted characters from E-CQDs were observed as shown in Fig. 4(aiii, biii). However, the green anti-counterfeiting mark (leaf) on banknote does not appear because the anti-counterfeiting mark (leaf) of banknote does not emit light under 405 nm. Through these results, we can verify that the prepared E-CQDs as anti-counterfeiting ink and film is difficult to mimic compared with the standard mark. To further confirm the stability of the written characters, the alphabets and circle were chosen as the model to be irradiated under a UV light (365 nm) and blue light (405 nm) for 10 h as depicted in Fig. 5 . The fluorescence images of written characters and film with different aging times are almost similar. The PL intensity of E-CQDs film with different aging times under UV light (365 nm) and in ethanol is no obvious reduction, indicating good photo and chemical stability as shown in Figs. S5 and S6. Through the above results, we can confirm that the E-CQDs can be useful for security ink and film as anti-counterfeiting application.

In order to verify the applicability of the D-CQDs as fluorescent flexible film, the photographs of CQDs solutions with different D-CQDs/C-CQDs ratios (1:0, 19:1, 9:1, 4:1, 2:1, and 0:1) under day light and UV light (360 nm) are shown in Fig. 6 (a). Fig. 6(b) appears the PL spectra of CQDs solutions with different D-CQDs/C-CQDs ratios (1:0, 19:1, 9:1, 4:1, 2:1, and 0:1) under 360 nm excitation. With the increase of C-CQDs ratio, the relative emission intensity of D-CQDs (blue region) becomes weak, whereas the relative emission intensity of C-CQDs (yellow region) becomes strong. The CIE coordinates of CQDs solutions with different D-CQDs/C-CQDs ratios (1:0, 19:1, 9:1, 4:1, 2:1, and 0:1) under 360 nm are displayed in Fig. 6(c). The CIE coordinates exhibit 1 (0.168, 0.133), 2 (0.200, 0.235), 3 (0.217, 0.283), 4 (0.238, 0.347), 5 (0.256, 0.392), and 6 (0.333, 0.518). It shows the significant shift from blue (D-CQDs: 0.168, 0.133) to yellow (C-CQDs: 0.333, 0.518) with increasing C-CQDs ratio. Fig. 6(d) reveals the photographs of the fabricated CQDs films with different D-CQDs/C-CQDs ratios (i. 1:0, ii. 19:1, iii. 9:1, iv. 4:1, v. 2:1, and vi. 0:1) under day light and UV light (360 nm). The emission color of the fabricated CQDs films is similar with CQDs solutions.

The D-, E-, and P-CQDs from waste paper as carbon source with different solvents (DI-water, EtOH, and 2-PrOH) were successfully synthesized by a conventional solvothermal method. The obtained D-, E-, and P-CQDs exhibit the pure structure and excitation-dependent emission. The prepared CQDs and films have excellent photostability and chemical stability. Upon exposure to UV light (365 nm) and blue light (405 nm), the optical property of CQDs and films almost does not change. The CQDs and films with a PVA medium offer high transparent ink and flexible film for anti-counterfeiting application. The emission color of CQDs solutions with different D-CQDs/C-CQDs ratios (1:0, 19:1, 9:1, 4:1, 2:1, and 0:1) alters from blue (0.168, 0.133) to yellow (0.333, 0.518). These results indicate a facile route to obtain D-, E-, P-CQDs and fluorescent flexible film with high photo and chemical stability and excellent optical properties. Therefore, we can solve the environmental problems through recycling of wasted papers and can apply anti-counterfeiting mark and flexible display using the D-, E-, P-CQDs and films.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea funded by the Ministry of Education (2017R1C1B1002434).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2019.123200.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of nitrogen-doping on the photophysical properties of carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Manioudakis, F. Victoria, C. A. Thompson, L. Brown, M. Movsum, R. Lucifero and R. Naccache, Effects of nitrogen-doping on the photophysical properties of carbon dots. J. Mater. Chem. C 7 (2019) 853.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Disentangling size effects and spectral inhomogeneity in carbon nanodots by ultrafast dynamical hole-burning

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Sciortino, M. Gazzetto, G. Buscarion, R. Popescu, R. Schneider, G. Giammona, et al., Disentangling size effects and spectral inhomogeneity in carbon nanodots by ultrafast dynamical hole-burning. Nanoscale 10 (2018) 15317-15323.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly biocompatible, fluorescence, and zwitterionic carbon dots as a novel approach for bioimaging applications in cancerous cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Sri, R. Kumar, A. K. Panda and P. R. Solanki, Highly Biocompatible, Fluorescence, and Zwitterionic Carbon Dots as a Novel Approach for Bioimaging Applications in Cancerous Cells. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces, 10 (2018) 37835-37845.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultraviolet to blue blocking and wavelength convertible films using carbon dots for interrupting eye damage caused by general lighting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. J. Park, H. K. Yang, B. K. Moon, Ultraviolet to blue blocking and wavelength convertible films using carbon dots for interrupting eye damage caused by general lighting. Nano energy 60 (2019) 87-94.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Invisible security ink based on water-soluble graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Song, T. Lin, L. Lin, S. Lin, F. Fu, X. Wang, et al, Invisible security ink based on water‐soluble graphitic carbon nitride quantum dots. Angew. Chem. 55 (2016) 2773.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 2D MnO2 nanosheets generated signal transduction with 0D carbon quantum dots: synthesis strategy, dual-mode behavior and glucose detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Gu, X. Li, Z. Zhou, W. Liu, K. Li, J. Gao, Y. Zhao, Q. Wang, 2D MnO2 nanosheets generated signal transduction with 0D carbon quantum dots: synthesis strategy, dual-mode behavior and glucose detection. Nanoscale 11 (2019) 13058-13068.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrahigh brightness carbon dot-based blue electroluminescent LEDs by host-guest energy transfer emission mechanism

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Xu, Y. Miao, J. Zheng, Y. Yang, X. Liu, Ultrahigh Brightness Carbon Dot–Based Blue Electroluminescent LEDs by Host–Guest Energy Transfer Emission Mechanism. Adv. Opt. Mater. 6 (2018) 1800181.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mussel chemistry assembly of a novel biosensing nanoplatform based on polydopamine fluorescent dot and its photophysical features

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Chen, Q. Wen, F. Gu, J. Gao, C. C. Zhang, Q. Wang, Mussel chemistry assembly of a novel biosensing nanoplatform based on polydopamine fluorescent dot and its photophysical features. Chem. Eng. J. 15 (2018) 331-338.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Malic acid carbon dots: from super-resolution live-cell imaging to highly efficient separation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Zhi, Y. Cui, S. Wang, B. P. Frank, D. N. Williams, R. P. Brown, et al, Malic acid carbon dots: from super-resolution live-cell imaging to highly efficient separation. ACS Nano 12 (2018) 5741-5752.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dot implanted graphite carbon nitride nanotubes: excellent charge separation and enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Wang, X. Liu, J. Liu, B. Han, X. Hu, F. Yang, et al, Carbon quantum dot implanted graphite carbon nitride nanotubes: excellent charge separation and enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. Angew. Chem. 57 (2018) 5765-5771.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green anhydrous assembly of carbon dots via solar light irradiation and its multi-modal sensing performance

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Lu, Y. Tang, J. Gao, Y. Chen and Q. Wang, Green anhydrous assembly of carbon dots via solar light irradiation and its multi-modal sensing performance. Dyes Pigm. 165 (2019) 287-293.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: White-emitting carbon dots with long alkyl-chain structure: effective inhibition of aggregation caused quenching effect for label-free imaging of latent fingerprint

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.-P. Jiang, Y.-X. Yu, X.-L. Guo, Z.-Y. Ding, B. Zhou, H. Liang, et al, White-emitting carbon dots with long alkyl-chain structure: Effective inhibition of aggregation caused quenching effect for label-free imaging of latent fingerprint. Carbon 128 (2018) 12-20.
